Cancer-associated fibroblasts condition the ovarian cancer microenvironment and therapeutic responses to antibody-drug conjugates and immunotherapy

High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) tumors are complex ecosystems where tumor–stromal–immune interactions shape therapeutic responses. Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) are major regulators in this dynamic crosstalk, yet their spatially distinct functional roles remain poorly understood. Using single-cell and spatial transcriptomics alongside patient-derived ex vivo models, we are mapping how CAF programs are organized at the HGSC tumor–stroma interface (TSI), and how this organization influences therapy sensitivity.

High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) tumors are complex ecosystems where tumor–stromal–immune interactions shape therapeutic responses. Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) are major regulators in this dynamic crosstalk, yet their spatially distinct functional roles remain poorly understood. Using single-cell and spatial transcriptomics alongside patient-derived ex vivo models, we are mapping how CAF programs are organized at the HGSC tumor–stroma interface (TSI), and how this organization influences therapy sensitivity.

Development and Optimization of High-Grade Serous Ovarian Carcinoma Tissue Processing: Focus on 3D Culture of Cancer-Associated Fibroblasts

Mid-phase presentation 13.1.2025
Aino Elomaa

Supervisors:
Assistant Professor, Anniina Färkkilä, University of Helsinki
Doctoral Researcher, María Hincapié Otero, University of Helsinki
Professor, Ullamari Pesonen, University of Turku

High grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC)

2

Created with BioRender.com

 Need for a deeper understanding of the HGSC’s biological complexity

Speaker notes:
most common subtype, accounting 70% of epithelial ovarian cancers

also the most aggressive subtype, typically diagnosed at advanced stages with metastasis to omentum and surrounding abdominal organs

despite initial response the treatment, relapse is common  around 25% of patients experience cancer recurrence within six months after treatment

highlights urged need for new therapeutical approaches  required deeper understanding of the HGSC’s biological complexity  and TME has gained increasing attention in its role in determining clinical outcomes

Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs)

3

Created with BioRender.com

Speaker notes:
Within the TME, CAFs are a highly heterogenous population

A high amounts of CAFs in epithelial ovarian cancer tumors is linked to advanced-stage disease such as the presence of lymph node and omentum metastases. 

CAFs contribute to tumor growth and behavior by promoting metastasis and angiogenesis, as well as affecting immune cell infiltration and modulating immune responses

Therefore there is a need to have a model that represents the TME and which incorporates CAFs

My thesis project aims to contribute to the Färkkilä laboratory's efforts to establish and validate a novel 3D tri-culture model of HGSC

4

Created with BioRender.com

Speaker notes:
labs aim to establish and validate a novel 3D tri-culture model of HGSC

The lab has already optimized an immunocompetent cell culture model that supports the growth of cancer and immune cells  but now the aim is incorporated CAFs

Aim is to use freshly dissociated tumor cells and embed them in omentum-derived gel to have a TME-representative model  This model could be used for drug testing to identify novel effective drug combinations and for biomarker discovery

My project aims to:

5

Speaker notes:
To remind, my thesis aims to

Preliminary results

6

Aim 1: Establish a standardized protocol for processing HGSC tissue samples

7

Speaker notes:
1) establish standardized protocol for tissue processing with the focus of successful dissociation of CAFs  testing 3 different enzymatic dissociations *** and evaluated the dissociation method based on quantifying the yield of CAFs, using flow cytometry

Gating strategy for flow cytometry analysis

8

Speaker notes:
Before showing the results, I will briefly explain how the flow cytometry data is analyzed:

First, I gate on cells to exclude debris, which are small particles or cell fragments not relevant to the analysis.

Next, I select single cells to ensure I’m analyzing individual cells, not doublets or aggregates.

From the single-cell population, I gate the live cells, excluding any dead cells.

Gating strategy for flow cytometry analysis

9

EpCAM+ = Cancer cells
CD45+ = Leukocytes
EpCAM- CD45- = Non-immune stroma
EpCAM- CD45- CD31+ = Endothelial cells
EpCAM- CD45- CD31- FAP+/a-SMA+/PDRGF-b+/CD29+ = CAFs

Speaker notes:
Within the live cells, I use EpCAM and CD45 markers to distinguish cancer cells (EpCAM+) and leukocytes (CD45+).

From the CD45-negative and EpCAM-negative population, which represents the non-immune stroma, I exclude endothelial cells using the CD31 marker.

Finally, from the CD31-negative population, I identify fibroblasts based on markers like FAP, α-SMA, PDGFR-β, and CD29.

This approach allows me to focus on the specific cell populations of interest while excluding irrelevant or overlapping populations.  then to the results

10

Enzymatic dissociation using Dispase and Collagenase & Hyaluronidase sequentially improves the yield of CAFs

Speaker notes:
By analyzing the frequency of FAP pos cell between dissociation methods and samples, we can see that the third dissociation method (Dispase and C&H sequentially) improves the yield of CAF 

On the x-axis you can see the different dissociation methods and within all samples, the sequential dissociation results in more CAFs.

11

Enzymatic dissociation using Dispase and Collagenase & Hyaluronidase sequentially improves the yield of CAFs

 Optimized SOP for HGSC tissue sample processing using the sequential enzymatic dissociation

Speaker notes:
So I have optimized a stadardized protocol for HGSC tissue processing which enables sufficient dissociation of CAFs

Aim 2: Characterize key cell types within the HGSC TME

12

Speaker notes:
2) characterize the key cell types  While quantifying the CAFs abundance within the sample using different dissociation methods, I also acquired data from the cellular composition within the samples

13

Frequencies of EpCAM⁺ cancer cells and CD45⁺ leukocytes across dissociation methods

* indicates p-value < 0.05

Speaker notes:
Looking at the frequencies of cancer cells and leukocytes  there are statistical differences between cancer cells when comparing the sequential dissociation to the individual dissociations 
BUT using the different dissociation methods does not significantly alter the yield of leukocytes

14

Frequency of CD31⁺ endothelial cells shows some statistical differences across dissociation methods

* indicates p-value < 0.05

Speaker notes:
Looking at the frequency of endothelial cells  again there is a some statistical difference but not between all dissociation methods

15

Frequency of CAFs shows statistical differences across dissociation methods

* indicates p-value < 0.05

Speaker notes:
Moving on to the CAFs  When again looking at the frequency of FAP pos cells, there is a statistically significance difference between the dissociation methods  sequential dissociation improves the yield of CAFs

Similarly with another fibroblast marker a-SMA, there is a statistically significant difference between dissociation methods


FAP = fibroblast activation protein
aSMA = α-smooth muscle actin
PDGRFb = platelet-derived growth factor receptor beta
CD29 = integrin β1: cell adhesion, migration, and signaling

16

Frequency of fibroblasts shows statistical differences across dissociation methods

* indicates p-value < 0.05

Speaker notes:
Also, PDGRFb and CD29 can be used to characterize CAFs

Again, similar results with PDGRFb as there is a statistically significant difference between dissociation methods

However, statistically significant results are not seen with CD29 but similar trend can be seen


FAP = fibroblast activation protein
aSMA = α-smooth muscle actin
PDGRFb = platelet-derived growth factor receptor beta
CD29 = integrin β1: cell adhesion, migration, and signaling

Aim 3: Enrich CAFs from HGSC patient samples

17

Speaker notes:
3) enrich CAFs from the acquired cell suspension using magnetic-activated cell sorting (MACS)

briefly about this technique: its used to isolate specific cell populations based on their surface markers. So here, the aim is to enrich CAFs by labeling cells with magnetic beads conjugated to antibodies targeting fibroblast-specific markers. When passed through a magnetic column, CAFs are retained, while unlabeled cells flow through. The retained, labeled cells can then be eluted from the column for downstream analysis.

Optimization of MACS protocol needed for effective CAF enrichment

18

Speaker notes:
Tried to enrich CAFs with this technique and here are the results. 

This figure shows flow cytometry plots illustrating the distribution of cell populations before enrichment, in the column-bound fraction, and in the flowthrough. The aim was to enrich CAFs which are part of the CD45 neg, EpCAM neg population. And theoretically the column-bound sample should include only fibroblasts.

Optimization of MACS protocol needed for effective CAF enrichment

19

Speaker notes:
Within the column-bound sample, the CD45- EpCAM- cell are only 45% of the cell suspension. This is suboptimal as optimally the yield of target population should be around 90 %

As seen in the flowthrough sample, a portion of the target population remains in the flowthrough. 

To conclude, this MACS protocol needs troubleshooting

Aim 4: Compare the growth and behavior of patient-derived CAFs in different environments

20

Speaker notes:
Compare the growth and behavior of patient-derived CAFs in different environments. Plan was to use MACS to enrich CAFs from the cell suspension 

 BUT since the MACS did not perform optimally, we decided to plate the dissociated cells in traditional 2D culture to enrich the CAF population.

Under traditional 2D culture conditions, CAFs tend to outgrow other cell types, making them the predominant population over time. 

And then I plated them in different conditions and compared CAFs growth in omentum-gel, Basement membrane extract gel and in suspension culture.

21

Suspension, Cultrex-gel and Omentum-gel support the formation of CAF spheroids

Speaker notes:
I plated CAFs in suspension and embedded in both omentum-gel and Cultrex-gel which is a type of commercially available BME. 

To see if the cell density effects the spheroid formation, I used 3 different cell densities  And monitored cell growth over 10 days

As can be seen, all three conditions support the formation of spheroid.

22

Lower concentration of Cultrex-gel advantage in spheroid formation

Speaker notes:
In addition to that, I wanted to see if the gel density effects the spheroid formations

Used Cultrex in three different concentrations, and observed, that using higher concentration of cultrex meaning a more dense gel the cells start to outgrow from the formed spheroid and a less dense gel supports the cells growth within the spheroid. Between the intermediate and low concentration Cultrex, the formed spheroids look similar and well structured.

23

Different Omentum-gel densities support the spheroid formation

Speaker notes:
When comparing different densities of omentum-gel, a similar outgrowing of CAFs from the gel droplet is not seen. However, the spheroid structure seems more consistent with the Cultrex-gel compared to spheroids in Cultrex-gel.

Evaluation of the project progress

24

Speaker notes:
To conclude the presentation, few words on evaluation of the project progress. 

I’m continuing with lab work with the third and four aim but the focus is shifting toward writing the thesis. I’m going to try the MACS sorting again with few alterations to the protocol. And overall, the results still needs to be evaluated to determine the final conclusions.

Thank you! Questions?

High grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC)

2

Created with BioRender.com

 Need for a deeper understanding of the HGSC’s biological complexity

Speaker notes:
most common subtype, accounting 70% of epithelial ovarian cancers
the most aggressive subtype
typically diagnosed at advanced stages with metastasis to omentum and surrounding abdominal organs. 
Omentum refers to this fatty tissue layer in the abdomen
first line treatment = surgery and chemotherapy 
despite initial response the treatment, relapse is common
around 25% of patients experience cancer recurrence within six months after treatment
highlights the urgent need for new therapeutical strategies 
this requires a deeper understanding of the HGSC’s biological complexity. 
in more detail, tumor microenvironment has gained increasing attention in its role in determining clinical outcomes

3

Tumor microenvironment (TME)

Created with BioRender.com

Speaker notes:
The TME refers to a complex and dynamic network of cellular and molecular components that supports tumor growth. 
Various cell types contribute to the TME: cancer cells, immune cells, and the nonimmune stromal cells including cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) , mesothelial cells and endothelial cells. 
soluble factors like growth factors and cytokines, as well as capillaries and the extracellular matrix (ECM)
all these are essential in shaping the TME and affecting tumor dynamics

4

Created with BioRender.com

Speaker notes:
Among TME components, cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) have been gaining increasing recognition with their importance in affecting the clinical outcomes

Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs)

5

Created with BioRender.com

Speaker notes:
CAFs are activated fibroblasts, exhibiting distinct cellular phenotypes influenced by their surrounding microenvironment 
A highly heterogenous population within the TME, and different subtypes on CAFs has shown to have different effects within the TME
but generally
A high amounts of CAFs in epithelial ovarian cancer tumors is linked to advanced-stage disease such as the presence of lymph node and omentum metastases. 
CAFs contribute to tumor growth by interacting with cancer cells and influencing the extracellular matrix remodeling. 
Then, the synthesis and remodeling of the ECM by CAFs further promote metastasis and impact angiogenesis, 
and changes in ECM composition can affect immune cell infiltration and module immune responses
And this all contributes to poor drug delivery and therapeutic response

Need to further understand the complexity of HGSC’s TME

Developing an accurate model of the TME enables the exploration of novel therapeutic strategies

6

Speaker notes:
Therefore: 
There is a need to better understand the complexity of HGSC and TME’s effect as it contributes to patients’ clinical outcomes. 
And to be able to explore of novel therapeutic strategies for HGSC, a model that represents the TME and biological features of this cancer is needed. 
One option is patient-derived 3D cultures

1) Establish a standardized protocol for processing HGSC tissue samples

Speaker notes:
then to the aims, Firstly, I will establish a standardized protocol for processing HGSC tissue samples. 
The lab has already optimized a tissue processing protocol that preserves immune cells, but further optimization is needed to ensure successful dissociation of CAFs
 I will test various enzymatic dissociation methods, including dispase- and collagenase-based approaches, and compare their effectiveness. 
The efficiency of each method will be evaluated by assessing the proportion of fibroblast activation protein (FAP)-positive cells using flow cytometry.
This will ensure that the obtained cell suspension maximize the yield of CAFs while maintaining cell viability and functionality

cancer cells
immune cells

my whole thesis project contributes to a bigger goal of establishing and validating a novel 3D tri-culture model of HGSC TME 
the model will include cancer cells, immune cells and CAFs in a immuno-competent patient-derived culture (iPDC). 
this iPDC model already successfully supports growth on tumor-originated immune cells but 
By adding stromal components to an immunocompetent 3D model, we aim to develop a more advanced system that closely mimics the complexity of the in vivo HGSC environment 
This model could be utilized for high-throughput drug screening and exploration of novel treatment strategies.

most common subtype, accounting 70% of epithelial ovarian cancers

Therapeutic focus in cancer is expanding beyond tumor cells to include the tumor microenvironment (TME)

Figures created with BioRender.com

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
currently the cancer research is shifting the focus from the cancer cell towards the tumor microenvironment to find novel treatments 
The TME refers to a complex and dynamic network of cellular and molecular components that supports tumor growth
All these components as illustrated are essential in shaping the TME and affecting treatment responses

4

Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) shape tumor behaviour

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
particularly the cancer-associated fibroblasts affect the tumor behavior
they are highly heterogenous cell population
A high amounts of CAFs in epithelial ovarian cancer tumors are linked to advanced-stage disease such as the presence of lymph node and omentum metastases. 
CAFs contribute to tumor growth by interacting with cancer cells, influencing the extracellular matrix remodeling and also modulate immune responses 
as illustrated in this figure
So as CAFs contribute to the treatment responses and in order to create new treatment strategies, a model that represents the TME and especially CAFs is needed

5

Current HGSC models lack non-immune stromal components 

 Incorporating CAFs and endothelial cells enhances the TME-representation

Figures created with BioRender.com

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
The lab has already a pipeline for establishing patient-derived 3D cultures that support the growth of cancer cells and immune cells
however, the model lacks the non-immune stromal components  to enhance the models TME representation and physiological relevance, stromal cells need to be incorporated 
so incoportating CAFs and endothelial cells to this model, is important as then the TME-representative model could be utilized for studying novel treatment approaches and to discover new treatment strategies HGSC

Optimization of CAF enrichment protocol

Next, as the second aim, a magnetic-activated cell sorting was optimized 
Two approaches were evaluated: 1) positive selection using anti-fibroblast beads, and 2) negative selection through depletion of cancer cells and leukocytes using a combination of anti-CD45 and anti-EpCAM beads. 
In both cases, cell recovery and viability were assessed, and enrichment efficiency was evaluated by multicolor flow cytometry.

Validation of 3D culture conditions for CAFs

18

Figures created with BioRender.com

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
lastly as the third aim, the aim was to see if the cells can be grown in culture after all the dissociation and enrichment process 
CAF growth and behavior were evaluated under different culture conditions: 
human omentum-derived gel, OmGel, which is produced from HGSC patients tumor-free omental tissue
Commercially available basement membrane extract BME
Suspension culture using ultra low-adhesion plates that supports the formation of a spheroid
and the CAF growth was monitored using light microscopy

19

Scale bar = 1 mm

All culture conditions supported spheroid formation

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
all culture condition supported spheroid formation
but the choice of culture conditions effects the cell spheroid morphology
Suspension culture requires minimal setup but lack the ECM 
BME or more specifically commercially available Cultrex is a BME matrix 
that is derived from mouse sarcoma cells. 
It consists of key ECM proteins and is widely used in 3D cell culture. 
But as it is mouse derived, it not the most physiologically relevant option. 
when increasing the BME matrix concentration, the spheroid stability is compromised
OmGel as human derived matrix 
offers the most physiologically relevant but also requires most effort 
Overall, it was validated that the patient-derived CAFs can be cultured within these conditions, but the key differences between the two matrices lie in their composition and the level of manual work required.


BME = composed of laminin, collagen type IV, entactin, and heparan sulfate proteoglycans

20

Optimized dissociation and enrichment protocols enabled robust CAF recovery for HGSC model development.
Sequential dissociation protocol ensured comprehensive recovery of key TME cell populations.
All tested culture conditions supported CAF spheroid formation, though matrix choice remains critical for future model optimization.

 This thesis paves the way for developing a more TME-representative HGSC model, with predictive potential for therapeutic development.

Key outcomes of this thesis

Introduction | Rationale | Aims | Materials | Results | Conclusion

Speaker notes:
First, I optimized both dissociation and enrichment protocols to allow for robust recovery of CAFs from HGSC patient samples. This was a key step in building a foundation for downstream model development.
Using a sequential dissociation approach, I ensured comprehensive recovery of key TME cell populations 
Then, I evaluated the three different culture conditions. While all supported the spheroid formation and growth, the choice of matrix is aa critical factor for future model development. 
Overall, these findings lay the groundwork for establishing a TME-representative model of HGSC. This novel model has predictive potential for disease modeling and could serve as a valuable platform for testing new treatment strategies.

22

Gating strategy for flow cytometry analysis

23

EpCAM+ = Cancer cells
CD45+ = Leukocytes
EpCAM- CD45- = Non-immune stroma
EpCAM- CD45- CD31+ = Endothelial cells
EpCAM- CD45- CD31- FAP+/a-SMA+/PDRGF-b+/CD29+ = CAFs

Speaker notes:
First, I gate on cells to exclude debris, which are small particles or cell fragments not relevant to the analysis.
Next, I select single cells to ensure I’m analyzing individual cells, not doublets or aggregates.
From the single-cell population, I gate the live cells, excluding any dead cells.
Within the live cells, I use EpCAM and CD45 markers to distinguish cancer cells (EpCAM+) and leukocytes (CD45+).

From the CD45-negative and EpCAM-negative population, which represents the non-immune stroma, I exclude endothelial cells using the CD31 marker.
Finally, from the CD31-negative population, I identify fibroblasts based on markers like FAP, α-SMA, PDGFR-β, and CD29.
This approach allows me to focus on the specific cell populations of interest while excluding irrelevant or overlapping populations.  then to the results

Using a sequential dissociation approach, I ensured comprehensive recovery of key TME cell populations 
Then, I evaluated the three different culture conditions. While all supported the spheroid formation and growth, the choice of matrix is aa critical factor for future model development. 
Overall, these findings lay the groundwork for establishing a TME-representative model of HGSC. This novel model has predictive potential for disease modeling and could serve as a valuable platform for testing new treatment strategies.

Thank you!

Gating strategy for flow cytometry analysis

23

EpCAM+ = Cancer cells
CD45+ = Leukocytes
EpCAM- CD45- = Non-immune stroma
EpCAM- CD45- CD31+ = Endothelial cells
EpCAM- CD45- CD31- FAP+/a-SMA+/PDRGF-b+/CD29+ = CAFs

Speaker notes:
First, I gate on cells to exclude debris, which are small particles or cell fragments not relevant to the analysis.
Next, I select single cells to ensure I’m analyzing individual cells, not doublets or aggregates.
From the single-cell population, I gate the live cells, excluding any dead cells.
Within the live cells, I use EpCAM and CD45 markers to distinguish cancer cells (EpCAM+) and leukocytes (CD45+).

From the CD45-negative and EpCAM-negative population, which represents the non-immune stroma, I exclude endothelial cells using the CD31 marker.
Finally, from the CD31-negative population, I identify fibroblasts based on markers like FAP, α-SMA, PDGFR-β, and CD29.
This approach allows me to focus on the specific cell populations of interest while excluding irrelevant or overlapping populations.  then to the results

vector_chunks.text:
Are the TSI fibroblasts associated with the immune exhaustion?

Fibroblasts in close proximity to the tumor–stroma interface (TSI), particularly myofibroblastic CAFs (myCAFs), are the main drivers of extracellular matrix (ECM) production, creating a dense, fibrotic barrier.
Does this matrix-rich stroma restricts immune cell infiltration and function near the tumor?
NK cells, B cells, CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, and dendritic cells are significantly more abundant in distant stromal regions than at the TSI. 
Overall, immune infiltration is higher in distant stroma, indicating an immune-excluded microenvironment at the tumor–stroma interface.

roenvironment and 
therapeutic responses to antibody -drug conjugates and immunotherapy
High -grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) tumors are complex ecosystems where tumor–stromal–
immune interactions shape therapeutic responses . Cancer -associated fibroblasts (CAFs) are 
major regulators of the tumor microenvironment, yet their spatially -segregated functional roles 
remain poorly understood . Using single -cell and spatial transcriptomics, along with patient -
derived multicellular cultures, we are investigatin g how CAF programs are organized at the 
tumor–stroma interface (TSI) and how they influence therapeutic responses .

FAP,PDGFRA, and PDGFRB in HGSC

PDGFRB and PDGFRA:
Some studies report that high stromal PDGFRB correlates with poor prognosis, but findings are inconsistent.
Based on IHC of PDGFRB and PDGFRA in stromal and tumor compartments in HGSC, PDGFRA and PDGFRB doesn’t have prognostic benefit in HGSC [1]. 
FAP:
High FAP⁺ fibroblast abundance alone was not associated with shorter survival in the whole cohort [2].
However, when both CD8⁺ T-cell density and FAP⁺ fibroblast levels were high, outcomes were significantly worse:
In the discovery cohort: 
High FAP + high CD8 → shorter PFS (11.4 vs. 18.6 months, p = 0.01) 
In the validation cohort: 
High FAP + high CD8 → shorter OS (31.5 vs. 76.9 months, p = 0.02)

Madsen, C. V., Steffensen, K. D., Waldstrøm, M., & Jakobsen, A. (2012). Immunohistochemical expression of platelet-derived growth factor receptors in ovarian cancer patients with long-term follow-up. Pathology Research International, 2012, 851432. https://doi.org/10.1155/2012/851432
Corvigno, S., Fernebro, J., Karlsson, J. S., Mezheieusky, A., Martín-Bernabé, A., Martin De La Fuente, L., ... Dahlstrand, H. (2025). High prevalence of FAP+ cancer-associated fibroblasts predicts poor outcome in patients with high-grade serous ovarian cancer with high CD8 T-cell density. Stroma-enriched CAFs (matrix-constructive phenotype):
ECM production: COL1A1, COL3A1, FN1, TNC, DPT, MGP

Chemokines/immune crosstalk: CCL19, CXCL1, CXCL3, CXCL14, PF4, PPBP 
Functional profile: ECM construction, immune recruitment, metabolic specialization 
TSI-enriched CAFs (activated, tumor-promoting phenotype):
Wnt pathway modulation: SFRP2, SFRP4 
Stress/immune response: IFI6, ISG15, DUSP1, EGR1 
Angiogenesis/remodeling: THBS1, TIMP3, MMP 
Functional profile: Activated, stress-responsive, angiogenic niche shapers
But! Some genes related to cell adhesion and matrix deposition also upregulated COL1A1, COL3A1, FN1,

Distant stroma CAFs: 
Matrix-constructive, metabolically specialized, immune-recruiting 
Likely support tumor growth via ECM deposition and immune modulation 
TSI CAFs: 
Activated, stress-responsive, pro-angiogenic 
Shape the tumor invasive front and aid therapy resistance

Instead of:

If TSI score > Stroma score → assign "TSI-like”

Else → assign "Stroma-like”

I used (thanks to Anna V.):

# Min-max rescale rescale01 <- function(x) {   (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) }

to rescale, so the lowest score becomes 0, the highest becomes 1, and all other values are proportionally scaled in between.

Full scale
Nika

Calculation for Normalized Enrichment Score (NES)

Raw enrichment score:

Average log2 fold change of each gene between TSI_high and Stroma_high is calculated
Genes are ranked by how much they are higher in TSI_high versus Stroma_high 
GSEA checks whether genes from each pathway tend to appear near the top or bottom of this list 
A running sum over the ranked genes gives the raw enrichment score (ES)

Random permutations: To know whether the observed clustering is unusual, GSEA shuffles the ranked list many times and recalculates the enrichment score for each shuffle. This creates a “null distribution” of scores we would expect by chance.

Normalization: The raw score is then divided by the average of these random scores (or scaled based on the null distribution) to account for differences in pathway size (big pathways naturally get higher raw scores) and the overall distribution of the ranked genes.

Also: NES direction shows which group the pathway is enriched in (Positive NES → enriched in TSI_high / Negative NES → enriched in Stroma_high), and its magnitude reflects how strongly and consistently the genes shift in that direction.

TSI-high cells formed a distinct cluster on the UMAP, separate from the Stroma-high cell cluster.

TSI-high cells formed a distinct cluster on the UMAP, separate from the Stroma-high cell cluster.

The Top100 and Top200 UMAPs look the same because both gene sets identify the same fibroblast subpopulation. This shows the signal is robust and not driven by a small number of genes.

TSI-like: ECM organization, TGF-beta signaling, high metabolism → “activated / tumor-promoting fibroblasts”

Stroma-like: Ribosome biogenesis, RNA processing, translation → “housekeeping / inactive fibroblasts”

GO Pathways TSI

KEGG Pathways TSI

All top enriched pathways point toward matrix remodeling and fibroblast activation, including ECM organization and cell-adhesion processes, ECM–receptor interactions, and signaling pathways that regulate proliferation, survival, and differentiation. All consistent with activated fibroblasts and tissue morphogenesis.

GO Pathways Stroma

KEGG Pathways Stroma

The stroma signature reflects a highly inflamed, myeloid-rich microenvironment characterized by strong cytokine/chemokine signaling (IL-17/TNF/NF-κB), neutrophil and myeloid recruitment, antimicrobial humoral responses, and ECM remodeling/degradation.

Both TSI and Stroma UCell scores are significantly higher in fibroblasts than in epithelial cells.

Epithelial cells have near-zero/zero medians, confirming that these signatures are fibroblast-specific.

Top100/Top200 vs TSI_UCell/Stroma_UCell (ρ = 0.8x) → The top variable genes across all fibroblasts and stroma overlap strongly with both TSI and Stroma signatures.

Stroma_UCell vs TSI_Ucell (ρ = 0.6x) → The TSI and Stroma programs are related but only moderately correlated, reflecting that they capture distinct aspects of biology.

For TSI:

Fibroblasts express the majority of TSI genes at higher levels, both in terms of fraction of cells and average expression, confirming the fibroblast specificity of this program.

Color intensity reflects the average expression of each gene in the cells of that type.

Dot size reflects the fraction of cells expressing that gene.

**Experimental Plan**

Experiment: Dissociation protocol optimization (\***Insert sample ID
here**\*)

Objective: Optimize the dissociation protocol to isolate more
fibroblasts from ovarian tumors.

Approaches:

1\. \*Using collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

- Condition 1: 20 min 0.125 % trypsin and 1 hour C&H

- Condition 2: 20 min 0.125 % trypsin and 2 hours C&H

- Condition 3: 20 min 0.125 % trypsin, 1 hour C&H, followed by brief
 > 0.125% trypsin of the cell pellet

- Condition 4: 20 min 0.125 % trypsin, 2 hours C&H, followed by brief
 > 0.125% trypsin of the cell pellet

2\. \*Using dispase followed by collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

- Condition 5: Dispase for 30 min, followed by C&H for 30 min

- Condition 6: Dispase for 30 min, followed by C&H for 1 hour

Experimental Steps:

1\. \*Sample Preparation\*:

- Collect ovarian tumor samples and wash the larger tumor pieces by
 > placing them into a 50 ml Falcon tube, pouring sterile HBSS on
 > top, and gently inverting the tube several times.

- Mince the tumor piece into small pieces using sterile scalpel and
 > scissors; very small pieces for C&H dissociation and bigger but
 > still small pieces for dispase dissociation.

- Divide the minced tissue into six equal parts for each condition.

\*Collagenase & hyaluronidase-only conditions\*:

for dispase dissociation.

 > bath.

 > Eppendorf tube per condition. Usually, 1x multicolor for each
 > dissociation condition, 1x unstained from one dissociation
 > condition, and then FMOs depending on the need and cell
 > availability. At least, DCM-FMO is recommended.

5. Wash cells once by adding 900 µL FACS buffer (2 % FBS, 2 mM EDTA in
 > PBS, stored in the wet lab fridge), centrifuge 6 min 400 g at
 > +4°C, and discard the supernatant.

 a. You may increase to 450 g if a pellet is not forming.

6. Prepare antibody mixes while centrifuging the samples: Surface
 > antibody staining

 a. For multicolor samples and a-SMA and PDGFRb FMO samples:

 i. Calculate the required amounts of antibodies (see table
 > above).

 ii. Prepare an antibody mix by diluting them in Brilliant Stain
 > Buffer (#563794, BD Biosciences, stored in the wet lab
 > fridge).

 iii. NOTE! Add the DCM last, just before pipetting into the
 > samples.

by diluting them in Brilliant Stain
 > Buffer (#563794, BD Biosciences, stored in the wet lab
 > fridge).

 iii. NOTE! Add the DCM last, just before pipetting into the
 > samples. DCM is not an antibody and may damage other
 > antibodies if stored in the mix for too long.

 iv. Flow antibodies found in "Antibodies for FACS box 2" in +4°C
 > wet lab fridge. Return the box immediately after use,
 > keeping the original antibody order. DCM can be found in
 > -20°C wet lab freezer.

 v. Add 50 µL of the antibody mix to each sample.

 b. For FMO samples:

 i. Add the required amount of Brilliant Stain Buffer to each
 > sample (see table above).

 ii. Add each antibody individually, one at a time, excluding the
 > antibody corresponding to the fluorophore being tested in
 > the FMO sample.

 c. Mix the antibodies and cells well with a pipette before starting
 > the incubation.

 d. Incubate for 30 min at RT shielded from light.

7. After incubation, wash by adding 900 µL FACS buffer, centrifuge 6
 > min 400 g at +4°C, and discard supernatant.

8. Fix cells by adding 200 µL IC Fixation buffer (#00-8222, Invitrogen,
 > stored in the wet lab fridge) and incubate for 40 min at +4°C
 > shielded from light.

9. After incubation, add 1 mL of 1x Permeabilization buffer
 > (#00-8333-56, Invitrogen, diluted in MQ water, stored in the wet
 > lab fridge) and incubate for 5 min at +4°C.

10.

9. After incubation, add 1 mL of 1x Permeabilization buffer
 > (#00-8333-56, Invitrogen, diluted in MQ water, stored in the wet
 > lab fridge) and incubate for 5 min at +4°C.

10. Centrifuge 6 min 400 g at +4°C, and discard supernatant.

11. Wash the pellet by adding 1 mL 1x Permeabilization buffer,
 > centrifuge 6 min 400 g at +4°C, discard supernatant, and resuspend
 > in 900 µL FACS buffer for overnight storage.

12. Next day, start by centrifuging 6 min 400 g at +4°C and discard
 > supernatant.

13. Prepare antibody mixes while centrifuging the samples: Intracellular
 > antibody staining

 a. For multicolor samples and FMOs samples excluding a-SMA and
 > PDGFRb:

 i. Calculate the required amounts of antibodies (see table
 > above).

 ii. Prepare an antibody mix by diluting them in 1x
 > Permeabilization buffer.

 iii. Add 50 µL of the antibody mix to each sample.

 b. For a-SMA and PDGFRb FMO samples:

 i. Add the required amount of 1x Permeabilization buffer to
 > each sample (see table above).

 ii. Add the remaining antibody to the cells: add a-SMA antibody
 > to the PDGFRb FMO sample and PDGFRb antibody to the a-SMA
 > FMO sample.

 c. Mix the antibodies and cells well with a pipette before starting
 > the incubation.

 d. Incubate for 30 min at RT shielded from light.

14. After incubation, wash by adding 1 mL 1x Permeabilization buffer,

s well with a pipette before starting
 > the incubation.

 d. Incubate for 30 min at RT shielded from light.

14. After incubation, wash by adding 1 mL 1x Permeabilization buffer,
 > centrifuge 6 min 400 g at +4°C, and discard supernatant.

15. Resuspend the pellets in 400 µL FACS buffer.

16. Pipetted the cell suspension through a 5 mL Falcon polystyrene tube
 > with a cell-strainer cap (blue) (#352235 Corning). Avoid bubbles.

17. Keep the samples on ice and protected from light until running on
 > Fortessa.

18. After running the samples, export the data as FCS files to a hard
 > drive.

Prepare compensation controls

1. Use Falcon 5 mL polystyrene round-bottom tubes (white cap) (#352052
 > Corning).

2. Prepare one unstained and 8 stained ones, one for each fluorophore.

3. Use BD CompBeads Plus (7.5 µm) compensation beads (#560497, BD
 > Biosciences, stored in the wet lab fridge).

4. Vortex the bead vials and add one drop of both positive (BD
 > CompBeads AntiMouse IgG, #51-9006274) and negative (CompBeads
 > Negative Control #51-9006227) compensation beads to each tube.

 a. Note! Ensure that the drops have reached the bottom of the tube.

5. For stained compensation controls, add 2 µL of each antibody.

 a. Except for a-SMA, for which 2.5 µL should be added.

 b. DCM cannot be used for compensation controls, so it should be

ained compensation controls, add 2 µL of each antibody.

 a. Except for a-SMA, for which 2.5 µL should be added.

 b. DCM cannot be used for compensation controls, so it should be
 > replaced with an antibody labeled with the same fluorophore
 > (BV510). For example, BV510 CD16 (#563829, BD Biosciences).

6. Incubate for at least 30 min at +4°C shielded from light.

7. Resuspend the beads in 350 µL FACS buffer.

8. Keep the compensations on ice and protected from light until running
 > on Fortessa.

9. Before running the sample, these compensations should be used to
 > determine the PMT voltages for each fluorochrome.

# Enrichment protocol to isolate CAFs 

This protocol concerns only the sequentially dissociated cells.
Meanwhile, keep the dispase-dissociated cells on ice.

suspension.

Explanations:

- Centrifuged = The plate was centrifuged after the content was added
 > to the wells, and the cells were not resuspended in the media/gel
 > solution. In addition, (1) the plate was kept cold so that the
 > Cultrex droplets did not start solidifying until after the
 > centrifugation, and (2) fibrinogen was added carefully to the
 > wells after centrifugation so that the ome-gels/fibrin matrices
 > didn't either solidify before centrifugation.

- Cell suspension = cells in media

- Cells in omentum = omentum diluted in media

- Cells in "normal" ome-gel = ome-gel prepared as previously

- Cells in ome-gel, 1.06 mg/ml fibrin = ome-gel, but volume of
 > fibrinogen, aprotinin, and thrombin was increased by x2.12

 > fibrinogen, aprotinin, and thrombin was increased by x5

- Cells in 1.06 mg/ml Cultrex = Cultrex diluted in media (NOTE!
 > Cultrex was previously diluted in DPBS)

- Cells in 2.5 mg/ml Cultrex = Cultrex diluted in media

- Cells in 7.5 mg/ml Cultrex = Cultrex diluted in media

- Fibrin matrix = same concentrations of fibrinogen, aprotinin, and
 > thrombin as in the original ome-gel, but diluted in media (no

**Experimental Plan**

Experiment: Dissociation protocol optimization

Objective: Optimize the dissociation protocol to isolate more
fibroblasts from ovarian tumors.

Approaches:

1\. \*Using collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

- Collagenase & Hyaluronidase (C&H) (10x stock, #07912, STEMCELL
 > Technologies, stored in the wet lab freezer Enzymes box) for 1
 > hour

2\. \*Using dispase followed by collagenase & hyaluronidase\*:

- Dispase (50 U/ml stock, #D4693-1G, Sigma Merck, stored in the wet
 > lab fridge) for 30 min followed by C&H for 1 hour.

- This approach includes dispase-only condition.

Media used in this protocol is DMEM/F12+++, which is DMEM/F12
(#11320033, Gibco) supplemented with 1x GlutaMAX (#35050061, Gibco), 10
nM HEPES (#H0887, Sigma-Aldrich) and 1% penstrep
(Penicillin-Streptomycin, 10,000 U/mL, #15140122, Gibco). Media is
premade in the cell culture room fridge.

Experimental Steps:

1\. \*Sample Preparation\*:

- Collect ovarian tumor samples and wash larger tumor pieces by
 > placing them into a 50 ml Falcon tube, pouring sterile HBSS
 > (#14175-053, Gibco) on top, and gently inverting the tube several
 > times.

- Mince the tumor piece into small pieces (using a sterile scalpel and
 > scissors on a 10 cm cell culture plate.

- Divide the minced tissue into 2 equal parts.

2\. \*Dissociation Protocols\*:

 > minutes at +37°C rotating. Vortex (approximately 15 seconds)

 > suspension through the filter into a 50 ml Falcon tube, wash
 > the stainer with 5 ml media, and **keep the tissue
 > leftovers.**

This document includes instructions on how to prepare stock solutions
for icPDC media, OmGel, dissociation enzymes, and dead cell marker (DCM)
used in flow cytometry.

# Stock solutions for icPDC media

**0.1% BSA**

To reconstitute certain reagents, a 0.1% BSA solution is required. Note
that the amount of 15 mg is based on the limitations of the available
scale, which cannot accurately weigh less than 10 mg.

Dissolve 15 mg in 15 mL DPBS (without Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺); the orbital shaker
in the cold room can be used to aid dissolution. Sterile filter with a
0.22 µm strainer before use.

**B-27 supplement Cat no. 17504044 ThermoFischer**

Aliquot 10x 1 mL per Eppendorf. Store at -20°C.

Working solution: 1x: add 2 mL of 50x stock in 100 mL medium

**β-estradiol Cat no. E2758-1G   Sigma/Merk**

Prepare stock solution 10mM: Dissolve 0.027 g of the powder in 10 mL of
absolute alcohol.

Aliquot 10x 100 µL and 9x 1 mL stock solution per Eppendorf. Store at
-80°C.

Working 100 nM: dilute 1 uL of stock in 100 mL medium

Peprotech **

BSA in DPBS (without Ca & Mg). Do not filter it.

Aliquot into 25x 40 μL per sterile tube. Store at -80°C.

**Fibrinogen Cat no. 341578-500mg Calbiochem/Sigma-Aldrich**

To prepare a 25 mg/mL stock solution: Add 10 mL of warm sterile mQ-water

L of warm sterile mQ-water and mix that carefully.

concentration used. The concentration was documented to be 25 µg/mL, and
then the working concentration was 0.5 µg/ml. In reality, the stock
solution was 25 mg/mL, and the working concentration should be 0.5 mg/mL
(as it is written in the Neilson et al., doi:
[10.1016/j.mbplus.2023.100136](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mbplus.2023.100136)).
However, the ratio is the same, so all iPDC cultures have been prepared
with the final fibrinogen concentration of 0.5 mg/mL.

**Dispase** **Cat no. D4693-1g** **Sigma-Aldrich**

Use sterile filtered (0.22 µm) 10 mM NaAc (sodium acetate, pH 7.5) to
dilute the dispase.

To prepare a 50 U/mL stock solution: Transfer the dispase powder to a
clean and sterile 100 mL glass bottle. Add 10 mL of 10 mM NaAc to the
bottle; use some of the NaAc to rinse the dispase stock bottle. Shake
gently for approximately 15 minutes using the shaker in the +4°C cold
room until the powder is dissolved.

Spatially-derived fibroblast signatures onto scRNA-seq-data – Correlation plots, part 2 –

Kürşat Birgin
Meeting, 03.02.2026

1

Aims

Does samples/patients with more exhausted CD8 or CD4 also have more TSI or Stroma activity?

Does samples/patients with for instance high tumor TROP2 expression tend to be associated with high TSI or Stroma scores in fibroblasts?

X-axis: Average UCell score for CD4, CD8, Expression TIGIT on CD8 or TROP2 on tumor (per patient/sample) 

Y-axis: Average UCell score for TSI or Stroma in Fibroblasts (per patient/sample)

Dataset

scRNA-seq dataset from Inga-Maria (Launonen et al.)

Spatially-derived signatures (TSI, Stroma, CD4, and CD8) from Nika

Gene expression information for TIGIT and TROP2 from the scRNA-seq dataset

Here: IDS/PDS combined, couldn’t find the time yet to run separately

We have 29 patients in total (so, 29 dots for the per patient correlation plots):

22 patients have 2 samples
7 patients have 1 sample

51 samples from 29 patients

Patients with 2 samples contribute 2 dots to the per sample correlation plots
Patients with 1 sample contribute 1 dot to the per sample correlation plots

Basic workflow

* It compares the rank/order of values rather than the exact numbers, so it’s robust to outliers. 
 Our gene expression and UCell scores aren’t normally distributed or strictly linear, so Spearman tells us whether higher values generally go with higher (or lower) values.

**Project introduction:** This project aims to elucidate how
cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer
(HGSC) influence responses to emerging anti-cancer therapies. To achieve
this, we will develop a novel 3D ex vivo model integrating
patient-derived HGSC tumor cells, tumor-resident immune cells, and CAFs
to more accurately recapitulate the complexity of the tumor
microenvironment. Using this model, we will then perform functional
drug-response assays to identify CAF-mediated mechanisms of therapeutic
resistance and uncover potential strategies to improve treatment
efficacy.

**Project lead:** María Hincapié-Otero, MSc (Doctoral researcher, 2023 -
present)

**Project members:** María Hincapié-Otero, Aino Elomaa, Sarah Wolf, Nika
Mikhailava, Kürsat Birgin, Sara Palomino, Foteini Chamchougia

**External collaborators:** Dr. Heidi Haikala, Bassel Alsaed (Haikalab),
Zac Darroch (Haikalab), Lilja Lahtinen (Haikalab)

SOP for BD FACS LSR Fortessa in B311 Kirsten/Xiaobo 
 23.03.2023 
 SOP for FORTESSA LSR 
 Booking the instrument 
 Reserve the instrument via online scheduler: 
 hp://w ww.super saas.c om/schedule/lt dk/V A360*000000001329 
 You can also reach the scheduler by scanning the QR-code 
 You may reserve the instrument after being trained and accepted by the contact 
 person. Contact nelli.heikkila@helsinki.i for more information. 
 Note: Please D O N O T adjust the ‘SAMPLE FINE ADJ’ on the control board. 
 Please D O N O T vortex the CST beads vial. 
 Prior to start 
 1. Write your name, date and the starting time on the log book 
 a. Check if the CST has been run by the previous user 
 2. Check the machine 
 a. Check that you have the correct i l t e r s for your setup 
 b. Check s h e a t h l u i d box (the left box). If it is empty replace it. New sheath 
 boxes available in B331. 
 c. Check waste box (the right box). If full or quasi-full 
 i. replace it with an empty sheath box . 
 ii. decontaminate the waste box by pouring 300-400mL of KLORILLI 
 into it and let it incubate overnight. Discard the waste to the sink, 
 recycle the carton box and discard the plastic container to waste. 
 iii. Press RESTART on the luidics carrier. 
 1

SOP for BD FACS LSR Fortessa in B311 Kirsten/Xiaobo 
 23.03.2023 
 Start the machine 
 1. Turn on the computer. User: Operator. Password: Welcome #1. 
 a. Open TERA Term Software, let it stay in the background. 
 b. Open BD Diva Software, login on your personal account. 
 2. Turn on Fortessa main power on (green button on the right side of the 
 instrument). 
 a. NB! The Fluidics carrier on the loor should always have its main power 
 ON. If it is not on, turn it on. 
 b. The laser warming up takes 30 min. 
 c. Check you have a tube with H2O in the sample needle. 
 d. When the instrument is turned on, it should on S t a n d b y mode in the 
 instrument front panel. 
 e. Remove the H2O tube. 
 f. Press Prime on the front panel (the low cell is emptied and you can see 
 bubbles in the waste line). Wait until the instrument returns to Standby. 
 Prime once more. (If you primed a tube loaded, discard the tube as it 
 may contain debris from the low cell) 
 g. Load a tube with 3-4mL of H2O. (Do not overill the tube.) Set the 
 instrument on R U N and H I . Allow water run on high low rate for at least 
 20min to make the CST pass. 
 h. If Diva does not connect Fortessa, try shutting down and restarting both 
 the instrument and the computer. 
 CST – Check the instrument stability 
 1. Set the low rate on LOW. 
 2. Check log book for any issues with CST 
 3.

ng down and restarting both 
 the instrument and the computer. 
 CST – Check the instrument stability 
 1. Set the low rate on LOW. 
 2. Check log book for any issues with CST 
 3. Open CST mode: Cytometer 🡪 CST from the menu bar. 
 4. Check you are using the coniguration corresponding to the ilters. 
 5. Inspect the previous Cytometer performance results 
 a. If the delta PMT values have a tendency of rising/falling during the few 
 last CST runs, contact Kirsten ( kirsten.nowlan@helsinki.i ) or Xiaobo 
 ( xiaobo.huang@helsinki.i ). The instrument may need service. 
 6. Get the CST beads that are currently in use from the fridge named ‘R2-D2’ near 
 Kirsten and Xiaobo’s desk. Prepare the CST beads: 350 µL of FACS Flow and 1 
 drop of CST beads. Tap the tube to mix. 
 Note: Please D O N O T vortex the CST beads vial. 
 2

SOP for BD FACS LSR Fortessa in B311 Kirsten/Xiaobo 
 23.03.2023 
 7. Choose C y t o m e t e r P e r f o r m a n c e from the drop down menu. Check you have 
 the correct bead lot selected. Load the bead tube. Click RUN. 
 8. When CST has passed 
 a. Load a tube of H2O. 
 b. View the report. 
 c. Put the instrument on STANDBY. 
 d. Get your samples and run them. 
 Short trouble-shoong for failed CST (by Marn Ivarsson, Karolinska Instutet) 
 CST sometimes does not pass. Don’t be alarmed, usually it’s simple to ix. 
 If FSC/SSC or other plots looked weird (eg. All events on the axis) you might not have 
 the right ilters in place. Check them and replace standard ilters (see sheet on the 
 paper tray above desk). 
 You should have about 200-600 events/second when running CST. If the event rate is 
 too low or high, adjust the low rate or the CST bead concentration accordingly. 
 You may also clean the low cell from air bubbles and debris: 
 1. Abort CST (keep the tube of CST beads) 
 2. Load a tube with H20 
 3. Prime 3x on the instrument front panel (any air bubbles in the 
 low cell will be purged). 
 4. Load a tube of FACS Flow and run HIGH for 5 min (reills the low 
 cell with liquid). 
 5. Load the CST bead tube again, set the low rate to LOW or 
 MEDIUM and restart CST. 
 6. Repeat if the problem persists. Also check the air ilter outside the

ll with liquid). 
 5. Load the CST bead tube again, set the low rate to LOW or 
 MEDIUM and restart CST. 
 6. Repeat if the problem persists. Also check the air ilter outside the 
 instrument as well as sheath and waste levels. 
 Other tips: 
 1. Make new CST beads with fresh FACS Flow or PBS 
 2. Clogging might be the reason: Run FACS Clean at HIGH low rate for 5 min and 
 then run H2O for 5 min. 
 3. Another tip from BD if CST keeps failing nothing else seems to work is to load a 
 tube with few mL of H2O and do the following: 
 ● Press Prime for 3 seconds. 
 ● Press Run for 3 seconds interrupting the priming. 
 ● Press Prime for 3 seconds. 
 ● Repeat 20-30 times. 
 According to BD, this will create a lot of turbulence in the low cell and remove any

SOP for BD FACS LSR Fortessa in B311 Kirsten/Xiaobo 
 23.03.2023 
 If CST problems persist, the issue may be in laser alignment or clogs. The instrument 
 may require service. Contact Kirsten ( kirsten.nowlan@helsinki.i ) or Xiaobo 
 ( xiaobo.huang@helsinki.i ) ASAP. 
 Shung down the instrument 
 1. Load a tube with 2-3mL FACS Clean and run it on HIGH low rate for 1 min the 
 holder on side of the tube and then for 4 min the holder under the tube. 
 2. Load a tube with 2-3mL FACS Rinse and run it on HIGH low rate for 1 min the 
 holder on side of the tube and then for 4 min the holder under the tube. 
 3. Load a tube with 2-3mL H2O and run it on HIGH low rate for 1 min the holder 
 on side of the tube and then for 4 min the holder under the tube. 
 4. Set the low rate on LOW. Set the mode on STANDBY. 
 5. Switch off the main power. (In case somebody is soon coming to run samples, 
 you may leave the power on. Switching on/off repeatedly may consume the 
 lasers.)
